# RAG Evaluation Harness — Authoring Guide

This fixture is the ground truth for the RAG evaluation harness introduced in
Story 9.1. The harness (`backend/tests/eval/rag/test_rag_harness.py`) measures
retrieval quality (`precision@k`, `recall@5`, `mrr`) and answer quality
(LLM-as-judge scores) against every labelled question.

Story 9.1 delivers the **instrument only**. It does not assert absolute
retrieval or judge thresholds — Story 9.2 captures the baseline numbers that
Stories 9.3 and 9.6 are measured against.

## File layout

```
rag_eval/
  README.md         ← this file
  eval_set.jsonl    ← labelled eval questions (strict JSONL, one JSON object per line)
  runs/             ← timestamped run reports from harness invocations (gitignored)
  baselines/        ← committed reference reports — one JSON + one .meta.json per embedding model
```

`runs/*.json` is gitignored — reports are generated by developers locally and
by the dedicated `ci-backend-eval` workflow, not committed.

## Baselines

`baselines/` holds committed reference run reports. Convention: one JSON per
embedding model, with a sibling `<model>.meta.json` reproducibility envelope
(corpus git SHA, seed row count, LLM models, harness version, run timestamp).
Stories 9.3 and 9.6 diff candidate embedding models against these files rather
than re-running the baseline embedding path themselves.

Current entries:

- `baselines/text-embedding-3-small.json` + `.meta.json` — captured by Story
  9.2 ([9-2-baseline-current-embeddings.md](../../../../_bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/9-2-baseline-current-embeddings.md)).

### Meta schema

Each `<model>.meta.json` MUST carry the following keys (the Story 9.2 file is
the canonical example):

- `embedding_model`, `embedding_dims` — model identity.
- `llm_candidate_provider` / `llm_candidate_model`,
  `llm_judge_provider` / `llm_judge_model`,
  `llm_fallback_provider` / `llm_fallback_model` — LLM path used during the run.
- `corpus_git_sha`, `corpus_file_count_en`, `corpus_file_count_uk` — corpus
  snapshot at run time.
- `document_embeddings_row_count` — output of `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM
  document_embeddings` right after seeding.
- `harness_version` — contents of the repo-root `VERSION` file at run time.
- `run_timestamp_utc` — ISO-8601 UTC; matches the filename embedded in
  `run_report_filename`.
- `total_rows` **and** `total_questions` — carry both (same value). The story
  AC uses `total_rows`; the run report emits `total_questions`. Future
  comparators should not have to guess which name applies.
- `error_rows`, `total_tokens_used`, `elapsed_seconds` — copied from the run
  report.
- `baseline_story`, `captured_by`, `selection_notes` — provenance.

### To add a new baseline (e.g. after Story 9.3 comparison)

1. Run the harness against the seeded corpus with the candidate embedding model.
2. Copy the chosen `runs/<timestamp>.json` to `baselines/<embedding-model>.json`.
3. Author `baselines/<embedding-model>.meta.json` matching the schema above.
   Minimal capture recipe (run from `backend/`, right after the run writes to
   `runs/<timestamp>.json`):

   ```bash
   git rev-parse HEAD                                         # corpus_git_sha
   ls data/rag-corpus/en/*.md | wc -l                         # corpus_file_count_en
   ls data/rag-corpus/uk/*.md | wc -l                         # corpus_file_count_uk
   cat ../VERSION                                             # harness_version
   uv run python -c "from app.db.session import SessionLocal; \
     from sqlalchemy import text; s=SessionLocal(); \
     print(s.execute(text('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM document_embeddings')).scalar())"
   ```

   Then copy `total_questions`, `error_rows`, `total_tokens_used`,
   `elapsed_seconds`, and the run timestamp from the run report verbatim.

4. Commit both files together; never commit a baseline without its meta.

## Running the harness

Cheap fixture/metric tests (default sweep, no LLM, no DB):

```bash
cd backend && uv run pytest tests/eval/rag/ -v
```

Full evaluation harness (requires seeded pgvector corpus + LLM keys):

```bash
cd backend && uv run python -m app.rag.seed        # one-time corpus seed
cd backend && uv run pytest tests/eval/rag/ -v -m eval
```

See `app.rag.retriever.retrieve_relevant_docs` (the unit under test) and
`app.agents.llm.get_llm_client` (the judge invocation path) for the exact
code-paths exercised.

## Schema — one JSON object per line

```json
{
  "id": "rag-001",
  "language": "en",
  "question": "If my income is 30000 UAH ...",
  "expected_doc_ids": ["en/budgeting-basics"],
  "topic": "budgeting-basics",
  "question_type": "applied",
  "notes": "Core applied question; synthetic author."
}
```

### Required fields

| Field | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `id` | string | Sequential `rag-NNN` — referenced by run reports |
| `language` | `"en"` \| `"uk"` | Must match a `{language}/*.md` file in `backend/data/rag-corpus/` |
| `question` | string | The natural-language query passed to `retrieve_relevant_docs` |
| `expected_doc_ids` | list[string] | At least one `{language}/{slug}` gold doc (e.g. `en/budgeting-basics`). Multi-doc allowed for cross-topic questions |
| `topic` | string | The corpus topic slug — must match a file in `backend/data/rag-corpus/{language}/{slug}.md` |
| `question_type` | `"factual"` \| `"applied"` \| `"definitional"` | Used for per-question_type aggregates in the run report |
| `notes` | string | Short rationale for why this question is in the set |

## Authoring methodology

### Why these questions

- **Core topics (20)** mirror the 20 canonical financial-literacy topics in
  `backend/data/rag-corpus/{en,uk}/` — see the topic list in the corpus
  directory itself (`ls backend/data/rag-corpus/en/`).
- **Supplemental topics (3)** cover Ukraine-specific context: `hryvnia-basics`,
  `ukrainian-tax-basics`, `monobank-ecosystem`.
- Every (topic, language) pair has at least one question, so the corpus topic
  coverage is exhaustive. The test `test_topic_coverage` enforces this by
  globbing the corpus directory — if the corpus grows, the test fails until a
  new question is added.

### Question-type mix

Target distribution (roughly):

- **factual (~40%)** — "What is X?" / "What are the main Y?"
- **applied (~35%)** — "If I spend ... what should I do?"
- **definitional (~25%)** — "Define X in one sentence."

Applied questions stress retrieval on paraphrased queries; definitional
questions stress precise-match retrieval.

### Choosing `expected_doc_ids`

1. Open the canonical corpus doc for the topic (`backend/data/rag-corpus/{lang}/{slug}.md`).
2. If the question is wholly answered by that one doc → `expected_doc_ids = ["{lang}/{slug}"]`.
3. If the question clearly spans two topics (e.g. "emergency fund vs savings
   strategies") → list both slugs.
4. Retrieval hit = `retrieved.doc_id ∈ expected_doc_ids` (any-match, not all-match).

### Redaction / PII policy

**All questions are synthetic.** They are authored by the developer, referencing
public financial-literacy content. They are not drawn from production user data
or real chat transcripts. This is intentionally different from the
categorization golden set (`backend/tests/fixtures/categorization_golden_set/`)
which redacts real Monobank statements.

Do not paste real user questions into this fixture. If you need a harder
adversarial corpus, add a separate fixture (e.g. for Story 10's chat red-team
corpus) — keep this set synthetic and public-content-based.

## Expanding the fixture

Whenever a new corpus topic is added to `backend/data/rag-corpus/{en,uk}/`:

1. `test_topic_coverage` will fail on the next test run.
2. Add at least one EN + one UA question for the new topic slug.
3. Re-run the harness locally against the newly-seeded corpus to confirm the
   retriever returns the new docs for those questions.

Whenever the harness surfaces a specific failure pattern (low `precision@1` on
a topic, language mismatch in judge output, etc.):

1. Add 2–3 more questions that exercise the pattern.
2. Do **not** remove questions to make a score go up.

## Interpreting a run report

Reports live under `runs/<timestamp>.json`. Each report contains:

- **Overall aggregates** — mean retrieval metrics, mean judge scores, total tokens.
- **Per-language** — `en` vs `uk` breakdown (detects bilingual regressions).
- **Per-topic** — one row per corpus topic slug (pinpoints topic-specific gaps).
- **Per-question_type** — `factual` / `applied` / `definitional` breakdown.
- **worst_10** — the 10 questions with the lowest judge `overall` score, with
  retrieved doc IDs, expected doc IDs, candidate answer, and judge rationale.

Story 9.2 will commit a reference report; future stories compare their run
against that baseline rather than against absolute thresholds.
